coredata.dc:title: Dual-wavelength ratiometric immunosensor for Bacillus cereus: Oxidase-like MnO2-Au trigged “OFF-ON” detection strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Jingnan
Shen, Haoran
Luo, Lin
Wang, Juan
Xu, Zhen-Lin
Liu, Yingju
coredata.dc:description: As an opportunistic pathogen, Bacillus cereus (B. cereus) has caused increasing emetic and diarrheal food poisoning, even fulminant hepatic failure and death case. Herein, a mimetic oxidase-mediated ratiometric fluorescence enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) was developed to detect B. cereus. In this system, 3D MnO2-Au nanoflowers with layered crystalline structure can oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to generate a fluorescence peak at 570 nm (I570, response signal), while blue carbon quantum dots (bCDs) display a fluorescence peak at 455 nm (I455, reference signal). Under the effect of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), the response signal increased while the reference signal was weakened. By using MnO2-Au as the mimetic oxidase to replace the horseradish peroxidase and associate with antibody in traditional ELISA, a linear relationship was established between the ratiometric fluorescence signal (I570/I455) and the logarithm of B. cereus concentrations in the range from 1.7 × 102 to 3.3 × 106 CFU/mL. Comparing with conventional sandwich ELISA, the detection time was shortened by 43 min and the sensitivity was improved by 5-folds, indicating that the proposed ratiometric immunoassay holds great potential as a rapid and convenient tool for sensitive screening of B. cereus.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacillus cereus
Mimetic oxidase
MnO2-Au nanoflowers
Ratiometric fluorescence
Immunoassay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for B. cereus
Detection of B. cereus in milk
Quantification of B. cereus by ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Guangdong Major Project of Basic and Applied Basic Research ( 2020B0301030005 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874048 ), the Key Project of Guangdong Provincial High School, China ( 2019KJDXM002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM and (B) TEM of MnO2-Au. Insert: size distribution of AuNPs. (C) HRTEM of MnO2-Au. (D) elemental mapping, (E) EDS, (F) FT-IR and (J) XRD of MnO2-Au.

(A) TEM of bCDs. Insert: (a) size distribution, (b) image of bCDs under 365 nm excitation, (c) HRTEM of bCDs. (B) FT-IR and (C) XPS survey of bCDs. The deconvolution of (D) C 1 s, (E) O 1 s and (F) N 1 s.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of bCDs at diverse excitation wavelengths. Fluorescent intensity of bCDs at different concentration (B) and pH (C). (D) The fluorescence emission spectra of bCDs, bCDs+OPD, bCDs+MnO2-Au, OPD+MnO2-Au, bCDs+OPD+MnO2-Au. Final concentration [OPD] = 1.67 mM, [MnO2-Au] = 0.017 mg/mL, [bCDs] = 0.42 mg/mL, and λex = 360 nm. (E) Spectra of fluorescence excitation and emission of bCDs, fluorescence excitation and UV–vis absorption of the OPD+MnO2-Au mixture. (F) Fluorescent lifetime of bCDs with or without OPD+MnO2-Au.

(A) Fluorescence responses for different concentrations of B. cereus. (B) the relationship between I570/I455 and the logarithm of B. cereus concentrations. Inset: linear curve (Left top) and the photograph (Right bottom) under 365 nm illumination of (A).
The response of the immunosensor on different kinds of bacteria (A). The enumeration results of B. cereus by the immunosensor and the MYP method (B).
Representation of the ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor for B. cereus detection.

head.ce:title: Dual-wavelength ratiometric immunosensor for Bacillus cereus: Oxidase-like MnO2-Au trigged “OFF-ON” detection strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: They are equally contributed to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an opportunistic pathogen, Bacillus cereus (B. cereus) has caused increasing emetic and diarrheal food poisoning, even fulminant hepatic failure and death case. Herein, a mimetic oxidase-mediated ratiometric fluorescence enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) was developed to detect B. cereus. In this system, 3D MnO2-Au nanoflowers with layered crystalline structure can oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to generate a fluorescence peak at 570 nm (I570, response signal), while blue carbon quantum dots (bCDs) display a fluorescence peak at 455 nm (I455, reference signal). Under the effect of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), the response signal increased while the reference signal was weakened. By using MnO2-Au as the mimetic oxidase to replace the horseradish peroxidase and associate with antibody in traditional ELISA, a linear relationship was established between the ratiometric fluorescence signal (I570/I455) and the logarithm of B. cereus concentrations in the range from 1.7 × 102 to 3.3 × 106 CFU/mL. Comparing with conventional sandwich ELISA, the detection time was shortened by 43 min and the sensitivity was improved by 5-folds, indicating that the proposed ratiometric immunoassay holds great potential as a rapid and convenient tool for sensitive screening of B. cereus.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Abbreviation: blue carbon quantum dots (bCDs), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), Bacillus cereus (B. cereus).

ce:section.ce:para: As one of three most important causative reagents of Gram-positive bacterial, Bacillus cereus (B. cereus) is widespread in different environments. It is considered as a threat to food safety due to the spore formation, the adaption to various conditions, and the production of harmful toxins including emetic toxin cereulide, hemolysin BL (Hbl), nonhemolytic enterotoxin (Nhe), and cytotoxin K (CytK) [1]. In addition, two types of food poisoning manifest itself as an emetic or diarrheal syndrome [2]. Furthermore, severe eye infections, osteomyelitis, and hepatitis, fulminant hepatic failure and even fatalities involved in B. cereus-related food poisoning have been reported. The increased prevalence of diabetes and impaired immune system are also closely related to cereulide exposure [3]. The contaminate rate by B. cereus was about 27% in pasteurized milk [4]. Therefore, the sensitive and rapid detection of B. cereus was urgently developed. Bacterial culture, biochemical assays, and nucleic acid-based diagnosis are predominant methods for B. cereus [3]. However, the time-consuming and requirement for well-trained personnel limited the application of these methods for routine rapid diagnosis. Antibody-based immunoassay technology has been successfully applied in many fields including medical healthcare, environmental monitoring and food safety [5], due to its rapid, convenient and inexpensive properties with specificity and sensitivity comparable to or better than the conventional method [6]. For instance, Zhu et al. reported an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) for the detection of B. cereus in linear range from 1.0 × 104 to 2.8 × 106 cells/mL [7]. However, some B. cereus-involved foods poisoning only contained 103 cells per gram of food [8]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop more sensitive immunoassay for the detection of B. cereus.

The ability of traditional ELISA that transforms the target concentration into color change depends on natural enzymes, such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP) or alkaline phosphatase (ALP) [9]. However, high cost and strict storage conditions still hinder their applications. Recently, nanozymes, the metal oxides with mixed surface valences, have attracted widespread attention due to its low cost, convenient preparation, and excellent stability [10]. As the most active catalysts among the transition metal oxides, manganese-based oxides, especially MnO2, are abundant in nature, indicating they are environmentally friendly and cheap [11]. It was reported that the catalytic oxidation activity of δ-MnO2 was the strongest in five different phases (α, β, γ, λ, and δ) [12]. The excellent catalytic oxidation activity of δ-MnO2 is attributed to oxygen vacancy on the surface or in the bulk of metal oxides and Mn vacancy [13]. Especially, compared to 2-dimensional (2D) materials, the unique 3D layered crystalline structure of δ-MnO2 can provide more active sites [14] for the deposition of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). Due to the perfect binding ability of Au with thiol groups or amino groups [15], Au NPs have been used to immobilize different proteins. Therefore, the presence of AuNPs on MnO2 enables the MnO2-Au composite to bind with the antibody directly without any modification, which greatly simplifies the preparation steps of the immunosensor.

Fluorescence immunoassay has aroused much attention due to its high sensitivity. However, the problems of poor repeatability and environmental interference were its intrinsic disadvantages [16]. In contrast, the ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay, conducting by calculating the ratio of fluorescence intensities of two well-resolved emission wavelengths under the same excitation wavelength, enables to eliminate the interference from the factors of target-independent, including the fluctuations of excitation source and light scattering [17]. Enzyme-mediated quenching of fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) (the oxidation products of o-phenylenediamine (OPD)) was the general strategy for constructing ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay [18]. Generally speaking, DAP was chosen as the response fluorophore in ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay, while the reference signal was from metal nanoclusters [19], carbon quantum dots (CDs) [20], and semiconductor quantum dots [21]. Among these fluorescent nanomaterials, CDs have attracted marvelous attention and widely applied in numerous fields due to its inherent advantages of the easy synthetic routes, favorable biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, good hydrophilic nature, and excellent tunable fluorescence properties [22].

In this work, a sensitive ratiometric fluorescent immunosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between blue CDs (bCDs) as donor and DAP as acceptor has been developed for the detection of B. cereus. 3D MnO2-Au nanoflowers with layered crystalline structure were used as the mimetic oxidase to replace HRP and conjugated with polyclonal antibody (pAb) in traditional ELISA. As in Scheme 1, at the excitation of 360 nm, bCDs was luminescent with a highly fluorescence intensity. In the immunoassay, B. cereus was captured by the monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) on the 96-well plate, followed by forming a sandwich complex of MnO2-Au-pAb. Since OPD can be oxidized as DAP by MnO2-Au, the photoluminescent of bCDs was turned “off”, while the DAP was turned “on”, causing a positive relationship between the fluorescence intensity (DAP/bCDs, I570/I455) with the B. cereus concentration. Compared with the conventional immunoassays, this ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay exhibited higher sensitivity and wider linear detection range.

bCDs were synthesized according to the earlier work [23] with minor modification. Briefly, 1.0 mmol diammonium citrate (226 mg) and 5.0 mmol urea (300 mg) were added into 10 mL deionized water. Subsequently, the above solution was moved into a 25 mL teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 7 h. The crude product was filtered by a 0.22 µm syringe filter and then dialyzed in ultrapure water for 12 h. The purified bCDs were freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for further use.

MnO2-Au nanoflowers were synthesized via a two-step method [24] with modification. Firstly, MnO2 nanoflowers were prepared. In a typical process, 6.3 mmol KMnO4 and 2.4 mmol MnSO4·H2O were added into 30 mL ultrapure water under vigorous stirring at 140 °C for 1 h. The crude products were cooled to room temperature with continuous stirring, and then rinsed triplicate with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol. The pure products were obtained by drying at 60 °C overnight. Secondly, AuNPs were decorated on MnO2 nanoflowers. Typically, 100 mg MnO2 nanoflowers were added into 100 mL ultrapure water and dispersed by ultrasound for 30 min, then 320 mg PVP and 128 μL 10% HAuCl4.3 H2O were added. The mixture was heated and stirred. After boiling, 200 μL 10% sodium citrate solution were added with continuous heating and stirring for 30 min. After washing triplicate with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water, the MnO2-Au precipitate was dried at 60 °C overnight and stored at 4 °C before use.

Then, the MnO2-Au-pAb probes were assembled via an electrostatic adsorption between MnO2-Au nanoflowers and pAb. Briefly, 2.5 μL of pAb (3 mg/mL) was added into 1 mL MnO2-Au nanoflowers (0.8 mg/mL) solution. The above solution was incubated at 25 °C with stirring for 30 min, then the 10% BSA (80 μL) was added to block the redundant binding sites and further incubated for 60 min. The MnO2-Au-pAb probes were separated by a centrifugation (4307g, 10 min) and resuspended in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.01 M, pH 7.4) with equal volume. The probes were kept at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for B. cereus
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the 96-well microtiter plate was filled with mAbs at equal volume (100 μL/well) and placed at 4 °C overnight. After washing twice by PBST (0.01 M PBS pH 7.4 + 0.05% Tween 20) to remove excess mAbs, the plate was blocked by 7% skim milk powder at 37 °C for 2 h, and then dried at 37 °C for 1 h. Successively, 100 μL of milk samples containing different concentrations of B. cereus cells were added at 37 °C for 30 min. The uncaptured B. cereus cells were discarded by rinsing with PBST for five times. Subsequently, 100 μL of MnO2-Au-pAb probes were added and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. After repeating the washing steps, 60 μL OPD (10 mM) and 60 μL bCDs (6.9 μg/mL) were added into every well and incubated at 40 °C for 12 min, the florescence intensity at 455 nm (I455) and 570 nm (I570) were recorded at the excitation of 360 nm, while the ratio of I570 to I455 (I570/I455) was calculated as the response signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of B. cereus in milk
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay in real sample was evaluated by spiking recovery tests and dynamic concentration assays. Milk was purchased from local market in Guangzhou, China, which was negative for B. cereus. The spiking recovery tests were carried out by spiking high concentrations of B. cereus in milk to achieve the final concentration of 1 × 103, 2 × 104 and 5 × 105 CFU/mL. The dynamic concentration assays were performed as the following procedure. The milk was artificially contaminated with initial concentration of 1.1 × 101 CFU/mL, and then detected every 2 h for 16 h. The enumeration of B. cereus test was executed and compared with the bacterial counts tested by B. cereus selective Mannitol-Egg Yolk-Polymixin (MYP) medium. The MYP medium plate method for enumeration of B. cereus in milk was carried as follow: the milk at different time was serially diluted in sterile saline, and then 0.5 mL was poured on the MYP medium plate and the concentration was calculated after incubation at 30 °C every 2 h.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images ( Fig. 1A) demonstrated that the morphology of MnO2-Au was a 3D flower-like nanosphere, while the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1B) implied that AuNPs with an average diameter of 22 nm (Insert in Fig. 1B) were distributed on the surface and fold of MnO2 nanoflowers. The HRTEM of MnO2-Au appeared the (101) plane of MnO2 and (111) plane of Au, which corresponded to the lattice spacing of 0.245 and 0.235 nm, respectively. The elemental mapping (Fig. 1D) of MnO2-Au nanoflowers showed that three elements (Mn, O, and Au) uniformly distributed in nanomaterials and the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectrum (Fig. 1E) also showed the characteristic peaks of Mn, O and Au, proving the deposition of AuNPs on the MnO2 nanoflowers. Furthermore, the atomic fraction (Table S1) of Mn, O and Au were 25.78%, 66.67%, and 7.55%, respectively. In the Fourier-infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. 1F), the peak of the O-Mn-O characteristic stretching vibration appeared at 540 cm−1, and the surface hydroxyl (Mn-OH) group of the hydrated metal oxide δ-MnO2 corresponded to the peak at the 1118 cm−1 [25], proving the existence of the MnO2 unit cell. The peaks at about 3414 and 1640 cm−1 were attributed to O-H stretching and bending vibrations by adsorbed water molecules. In the X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) of MnO2-Au nanoflowers (Fig. 1G), the Bragg reflections at 12.1°, 24.7°, 36.7°, 65.9° were corresponded to (003), (006), (101), (110) crystal planes of MnO2 (JCPDS No.52–0556) [24], and the Bragg reflections at 38.2°, 44.4°, 64.6°, 77.6° matched with (111), (200), (220), (311) crystal planes of Au (JCPDS No. 65–8601) [26].

The surface elemental compositions were further characterized with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), where the survey spectra of MnO2-Au showed three characteristic absorption peaks at 643, 530, and 85 eV corresponding to the binding energy of Mn 2p, O 1 s, and Au 4 f, respectively (Fig. S1A), agreeing with EDS mapping. The chemical states of MnO2-Au nanoflowers were also investigated. The binding energy at 654.2 eV and 642.3 eV in high-resolution spectra of Mn 2p were corresponded to Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 (Fig. S1B), indicating that + 4 was the predominant oxidation state of Mn atoms in MnO2-Au [27]. The Au 4 f peaks at 88.1 and 84.4 eV (Fig. S1C) belonged to metallic Au0, which indicated the presence of atomic Au on MnO2 [28]. In the O 1 s spectrum, the peaks at 531.7 eV and 529.9 eV were assigned to the surface-absorbed component and bulk-coordinated oxygen, respectively [27].

The oxidase mimetic activity of MnO2-Au was evaluated by the steady-state kinetic experiment by using OPD as the substrate. The MnO2-Au can directly oxidize colorless OPD into yellow DAP without adding H2O2. As shown in Fig. S2A and B, the typical Michaelis-Menten reaction was displayed by the OPD concentration and initial velocity. The K m value for MnO2-Au with OPD as the substrate was calculated as 0.22 mM by the double-reciprocal Lineweaver-Burk plots, indicating that MnO2-Au nanoflowers possessed high oxidase mimetic activity.

After that, the MnO2-Au nanoflowers were used to immobilize pAb, which were characterized with zeta potential and UV–vis spectra. Fig. S3A showed that the MnO2 was − 20 mV, while the decoration of Au NPs can change the zeta potential to − 16 mV, and the further conjugation with pAb decreased substantially to − 6 mV. In addition, in the UV–vis spectra, no adsorption peak of MnO2-Au was found, but an absorption peak at 280 nm attributed to protein was present in the spectra of MnO2-Au-pAb [29] (Fig. S3B), confirming the signal probe was successfully constructed.

The morphology of the bCDs was characterized with TEM ( Fig. 2A), where uniformly dispersive waterdrop shapes with an average diameter of 1.75–2.5 nm were discovered (the inset a of Fig. 2A). The bCDs emitted dazzling blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation (the inset b of Fig. 2A). Especially, the HRTEM image of bCDs showed an obvious lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which was attributed to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [30] (the inset c of Fig. 2A). The functional groups of bCDs were further demonstrated by the FT-IR absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 2B). The absorption peaks at 3430 and 1630 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of O-H and CO, respectively. The characteristic absorption peak of CC was appeared at 1587 cm−1. The stretching and the bending vibrations of the C-H bond appeared at 2887 and 1280 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at lower frequency denoted O-H bending peak (1342 cm−1) and C-O stretching peak (1108 cm−1), respectively. Therefore, bCDs were surrounded by -NH2, -COOH and -OH groups [23]. These functional groups including -NH2 and -OH, as the electron donating auxochrome groups, contributed to the enhancement of fluorescence intensity [31].

The surface states of bCDs were probed by XPS characterization, where three sharp peaks presented in the full-scan survey, corresponding to the binding energy of O 1 s, N1s, and C 1 s (Fig. 2C). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum were deconvoluted into four peaks at 288.5, 286.3, 285.4, and 284.8 eV, attributing to the bonds of CO, C-OH, C-N, and C-C, respectively [32] (Fig. 2D). Three fitted peaks at 533.2, 532.5, and 531.7 eV of O 1 s were assigned to C-O, CO, and O-H bonds, respectively (Fig. 2E). The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum indicated that three chemical states of pyrrolic N (400.2 eV), amino N (399.5 eV), and pyridinic N (398.9 eV) (Fig. 2F) were discovered.

Firstly, optical characteristics of the bCDs were elaborated. Fig. 3A showed that the fluorescence emission spectra of bCDs had no concern with the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength was at 360 nm, the fluorescent intensity of bCDs was the greatest. The concentration of bCDs can also influence the fluorescence of bCDs. Generally, in a dilute solution, the fluorescence intensity is positively correlated with the concentration of the fluorescent substance. However, if the concentration is too high, the self-adsorption of the fluorescent substance is inevitable, and eventually a decrease in fluorescence intensity may be found [33]. Fig. 3B indicated that 1.25 mg/mL of bCDs had the strongest fluorescence emission. Therefore, 1.25 mg/mL was selected as a stock concentration of bCDs. Furthermore, the pH of the detection solution can also influence the fluorescence. As in Fig. 3C, bCDs only had strong fluorescence emission in neutral environment (pH = 7.0), and the fluorescence intensity decreased under acidic (pH = 2.0–6.0) and alkaline (pH = 8.0–9.0) conditions. The protonation of -NH2 and deprotonation of -COOH/-OH were responsible for this phenomenon [34]. When the solution was under the acidic condition, the -NH2 group, as the electron donating auxochrome groups, was protonated. Subsequently, it had no free electron to donate, so the fluorescence emission intensity was remarkably affected. Similarly, the -OH group was deprotonated under alkaline condition, resulting in losing of electron supply capability. The deprotonation of the -COOH group also weaken the electron-capturing capabilities [35]. Therefore, the pH value of 7.0 was selected.

After that, the FRET between bCDs as donor and DAP as acceptor was investigated. As in Fig. 3D, the individual MnO2-Au or OPD cannot significantly change of bCD's fluorescence. In addition, OPD cannot emit any fluorescence under the excitation at 360 nm, but when MnO2-Au was mixed with OPD, strong fluorescence peak of DAP appeared at 570 nm, indicating the MnO2-Au can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of OPD. The emission intensity of bCDs was significantly quenched by introducing MnO2-Au into the OPD/bCDs solution, which resulted in a ratiometric fluorescence response. Furthermore, the quenching mechanism of bCDs by the mixture of OPD+MnO2-Au was explored. As shown in Fig. 3E, both the spectra of fluorescence excitation and UV–vis absorption band of OPD+MnO2-Au mixture greatly overlapped with the fluorescence emission band of bCDs, which satisfied the basic conditions for triggering the FRET. To confirm the effect, the fluorescence lifetime of bCDs with or without OPD/MnO2-Au was evaluated. As in Fig. 3F, the fluorescence lifetime of bCDs before and after the addition of MnO2-Au+OPD were 5.17 ns and 3.90 ns, respectively. It was reported that the fluorescence lifetime was easily affected by the FRET process but not obviously affected by inner filter effect (IFE) [32,36]. In a conclusion, the FRET between bCDs and OPD/MnO2-Au was responsible for the quenching of bCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of B. cereus by ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental parameters, including pH value, incubation temperature, the concentrations of OPD, incubation time, and working concentration of bCDs, were optimized (Supporting information, Figs. S4 and S5). In the immunoassay, the mAbs were pre-adsorbed onto the 96-well plate to capture the B. cereus, followed by forming a sandwich complex with the MnO2-Au-pAb. Therefore, a higher concentration of B. cereus can induce a higher amount of MnO2-Au-pAb, which can realize a higher FRET. Accordingly, the ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay in the presence of a series of B. cereus concentrations and the corresponding calibration plot for the B. cereus measurement were demonstrated in Fig. 4A and B. As in Fig. 4A, the introduction of B. cereus can quench the fluorescence of bCDs and also enhance the fluorescence of DAP. When the B. cereus concentration increased from 5.6 × 101 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL, the fluorescence intensity of DAP (570 nm) and bCDs (455 nm) gradually increased and decreased, respectively. The linear relationship between I570/I455 (y) and the logarithm of the concentrations of B. cereus (x) was fit to the equation y = 0.89x-1.41 in the range of 1.7 × 102 to 3.3 × 106 CFU/mL with an excellent correlation coefficient of 0.99 (Fig. 4B). The detection limit for B. cereus was calculated as 1.7 × 102 CFU/mL. Comparing with conventional double antibody sandwich ELISA, the detection time of proposed strategy was shortened as 43 min and the sensitivity was improved by about 5-folds (LOD: 9 ×102 CFU/mL) [7]. The linear detection range and LOD of this work were better or comparable with the reported assays (Table S2). In addition, different concentrations of B. cereus cells were directly diluted by milk to build the standard curve. Therefore, there was no matrix effects during the detection. The low detection limit and the broad linear range allowed it as a highly sensitive approach for the determination of B. cereus. Moreover, the sensing platform can be extended for the detection of different foodborne pathogens by introducing of the corresponding antibody, which provides a convenient way for monitoring food safety.

To confirm whether ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor can detect B. cereus selectively, the fluorescence response of the probe toward B. cereus (1.0 × 105 CFU/mL) and non-target bacteria (1.0 × 107 CFU/mL) were recorded ( Fig. 5A). The non-target bacteria including B. cereus group strains (B. thuringiensis and B. mycoides) and other foodborne pathogens (B. megaterium, B. licheniformis, S. aureus, E. coli, and so on) were provided in the supporting information. Though the concentrations of non-target bacteria were 100 folds of the target bacteria, the results showed that the ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor exhibited negligible response not only with other foodborne pathogens but also with other bacteria from B. cereus group, but showed significant response toward B. cereus. In addition, to further explore the specificity of immunosensor, B. thuringiensis was selected to build mixture system with B. cereus. As in Fig. S6, even though the concentration of B. thuringiensis in the mixture system increased from 101 to 106 CFU/mL, the response of the immunosensor toward B. cereus showed negligible changes, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor was fantastically specific for B. cereus detection.

After that, the stability of the immunosensor was assessed. The fluorescence responses of the probe toward B. cereus at 1.0 × 105 CFU/mL were recorded at different time (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 14 days). As in Fig. S7, the values of I570/I455 were slightly fluctuated with the increase in time. The excellent stability of immunosensor might be attributed to the superior chemical stability and good compatibility of MnO2-Au.

The feasibility and applicability of ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor were evaluated by using milk as the model sample. As selection medium for B. cereus, MYP medium is widely applied in enumeration and identification of B. cereus [37]. Therefore, it can be as verification method for the ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor. The commercially available milk was initially proved as negative by bacterial culture method using MYP medium. The recoveries of B. cereus of three spiked concentration of 1 × 103, 2 × 104 and 5 × 105 CFU/mL were 98.8%, 95.5% and 93.8%, respectively, while the coefficients of variation were 5.1%, 4.2% and 6.5%, respectively. Compared to spiked recovery test, the dynamic concentration assays can better measure the performance of the immunosensor. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, though our method was not able to accurately count the initial spiked concentration, the enumeration results of B. cereus by our method maintained high consistency with the MYP method in range from 1.8 × 102 to 3.3 × 106 CFU/mL, indicating that the proposed method had great potential in the detection of B. cereus in real food samples. Moreover, the developed ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor can be used to detect other bacteria or biomarkers by introducing the responding antibody.

In summary, the 3D MnO2-Au nanoflowers with mimetic oxidase were conjugated with pAb as an immunoprobe. Inspired by the oxidization of OPD with MnO2-Au, the product DAP can quench the fluorescence of bCDs. The ratiometric immunoassay via FRET between DAP as acceptor and bCDs as donor was developed for the detection of B. cereus. Under optimal conditions, this method exhibited excellent detection performance with the linear range from 1.7 × 102 to 3.3 × 106 CFU/mL. Given the selectivity, sensitivity and convenience of this immunosensor, it may hold great promise for detection of B. cereus in food safety. This strategy has opened new orientation for improving traditional and available detection methods with high selectivity, more sensitivity and short time analysis.

Jingnan Meng: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing − original draft. Haoran Shen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Lin Luo: Investigation. Juan Wang: Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Zhen-Lin Xu: Conceptualization, Writing − review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yingju Liu: Conceptualization, Writing − review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Guangdong Major Project of Basic and Applied Basic Research (2020B0301030005), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874048), the Key Project of Guangdong Provincial High School, China (2019KJDXM002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131925.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance testing of Bacillus cereus chromogenic agar media for improved detection in milk and other food samples
Foods

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jingnan Meng received her B.S. degree in 2016 and Master degree in 2019 from College of Food Science and Technology, Henan Agricultural University, China. She is currently working on the construction of biosensors for Bacillus cereus in foods.

Haoran Shen received his B.S. degree in 2019 and Master degree in 2021 from College of Material and Energy, South China Agricultural University, China. His primary research interests focus on the construction of biosensor and functional nanomaterial.

Lin Luo obtained his B.S. degree from Hunan Agricultural University in 2011 and Ph.D. degree from South China Agricultural University in 2017. He is committed to the research and application of rapid detection technology for food quality and safety.

Juan Wang received her B.S. degree in 2006 form Sun Yat-Sen University and Ph.D. in philosophy in 2016 from the Chinese University of Hong Kong. She is currently working on high-throughput rapid detection of food-borne pathogenic microorganisms and research on the mechanism of hazard formation.

Zhen-Lin Xu obtained his B.S. degree in 2004 and Ph.D. degree in 2011 from College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, China. His research interests focus on basic and applied research on rapid detection of food safety.

Yingju Liu received her B.S. degree in 2000 and Ph.D. in chemistry in 2005 from Hunan University. She is currently a full-time professor in the Department of Applied Chemistry, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, China. Her primary research interests concern the biosensor and nanomaterials.
